title: Langues de chat
subtitle: Very thin, crisp French finger biscuits
excerpt: Langues de chat are slim, pale, crisp biscuits piped into finger shapes and baked until the edges are golden and the centres are just set. The name means 'cat's tongues', from their flat, oblong shape. They are the classic accompaniment to mousse, ice cream, and champagne. The batter is quick to make and pipes well from a plain nozzle.
sourceNotes: Langues de chat are a traditional French patisserie biscuit, documented in French domestic cookery from the nineteenth century onward. The formula is simple and standard: equal weights of butter, sugar, and flour, with egg whites for structure. Firmly public domain.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily; batter keeps for 30 minutes at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin for up to 3 days. They soften in humidity.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; they colour quickly at the edges

content.text: Langues de chat are very thin, crisp finger biscuits piped from a buttery batter of equal weights of butter, sugar, and flour. They are pale gold with slightly darker edges, light, and delicate; an accompaniment to desserts rather than a stand-alone biscuit. The batter is beaten together in one bowl and piped immediately; the whole process from ingredients to oven takes under 10 minutes.

Beat the softened butter and icing sugar together until pale and fluffy. Add the vanilla. Add the egg whites a little at a time, beating well between each addition. The mixture may look slightly curdled; fold in the sifted flour and it will come back together. The batter should be smooth and pipeable.

Heat the oven to 190°C fan. Line baking trays with baking paper. Fit a piping bag with a 5 mm plain nozzle and pipe 5 cm fingers onto the tray, spacing them at least 4 cm apart as they spread. Bake for 7 to 9 minutes. The biscuits are done when the edges are pale gold and the centres are just set. They firm as they cool. Cool on the tray for 2 minutes before lifting; they are fragile while warm.
